description: This figure presents a regression analysis chart illustrating the influence of chemical composition on the elastic modulus of martensitic and ferritic steels, with phase composition and microstructure neglected. The horizontal axis lists alloying elements sorted by decreasing statistical significance (r_i/σ_i): Fe, Cr, Mo, Si, Ni, Mn, Cu, and C. The vertical axis displays the regression coefficient r_i in GPa, quantifying each element's contribution per mass percent to the elastic modulus. Solid black squares with error bars represent the regression coefficients derived from this study (error bars indicate standard errors), while open circles denote corresponding literature data points. The chart reveals that Si exhibits the highest positive regression coefficient (~4.6 GPa), followed by Cr (~2.7 GPa), Mo (~2.6 GPa), and Cu (~2.4 GPa). In contrast, C shows a negative coefficient (~-0.5 GPa), indicating a reduction in elastic modulus, while Ni demonstrates a negligible to slightly negative effect. Mn and Fe display modest positive contributions. As stated in the caption, this analysis encompasses 53 batches of steel; Cr, Mo, and Si significantly increase the elastic modulus, whereas C and Ni decrease it, with Mn and Cu being statistically insignificant in the investigated concentration ranges. The regression model exhibits a standard error of 1.2 GPa and an R² value of 0.96.
